chunks.text: Intro  Now see the importing, we have seen how it is done in python programming  If I import any function from one module to another module  Or suppose I made a file named harry.py and made a file named main.py  I imported harry.py in main.py

chunks.title: if
chunks.text: Now what will happen is that if some functions are being executed in harry.py  then they will be executed automatically by importing  I will show you  So see, I have made my own repl here  I do one thing quickly, I write import harry here

chunks.title: if
chunks.text: I am going to make a file named harry, so don't worry  Now what I will do is I will simply print  Or leave print, there will be a function named harry.welcome  Which will welcome people  So I do one thing, I make a harry.py

chunks.title: if
chunks.text: And see here, hey you are welcome from harry is printed  Because I have made a function and executed it  There is no rocket science  Now see here, import harry is written and harry.welcome is written  So here you can find out rocket science and differential equation

chunks.title: if
chunks.text: What will happen here  I am joking, there is no rocket science  You will feel that by importing harry, all functions will be imported  And by running harry.welcome, hey you are welcome will be printed again  So if I do python main.py

chunks.title: if
chunks.text: So to your surprise, this thing is printed twice  And this is printed twice because  Once we are doing, once it is expected  That you have done, you have written harry.welcome  So it has to be executed

chunks.title: if
chunks.text: But why is it happening the second time  Because by importing harry, def welcome and welcome is also executed  Means if I only import harry  So any code in harry.py will be run  Answer is yes, it will be run

chunks.title: if
chunks.text: How do I solve this problem, how do I avoid this  This is a very big problem  This is a very big problem, by importing harry  This is executing harry.welcome  This is a very big problem, how to solve this

chunks.title: if
chunks.text: I will write here if __name == __main  Now see here there is double quote  Here it is not there, remember this  So basically I am saying here  If I run it from here, then run it

chunks.title: if
chunks.text: Otherwise don't run it  If I run main.py, then it will be run only once  Hey you are welcome harry  I will run it again in shell  If I write here python harry.py

chunks.title: if
chunks.text: Then also it will be run once  And if I write python main.py  Then also it will be run once  So how is this possible for me  I have put main in harry.py if name ==

chunks.title: if
chunks.text: If I print here only __name  What is __name  What is __name  __name means  From where it is being printed

chunks.title: if
chunks.text: Now it is main, it means it is being run from here  This program is being run from here  That's why it is being written main  But if I run main.py  Then what will happen, it will be harry

chunks.title: if
chunks.text: That is, it is being run after importing harry  So here __name will be harry  If I run it directly like this  Python harry.py  Then __name value will be main

chunks.title: if
chunks.text: If I run it from somewhere else  Then __name value will be harry  So I put a check basically  If __name value is __main__  It means it is being run from here

chunks.title: if
chunks.text: And because it is being run from here  That's why we will run welcome  But if it is not being run from here  Then we will not run welcome  Okay, you understood

chunks.title: if

chunks.title: if
chunks.text: This script is being run directly  Or it is being run as a result  Of being imported from some other module  Okay  You have imported it in some other script

chunks.title: if

chunks.title: if

chunks.title: if

chunks.text: Why is it useful?  Is it necessary to use it every time?  If you think  The file in which you are working  You can import it from somewhere else

chunks.title: if
chunks.text: Then it is necessary  Because otherwise  If you import harry.py  From somewhere else  Like I imported it from main

chunks.title: if
chunks.text: Then there will be such problems  That welcome will automatically run  And if there is any critical function  Then you will be banned  Really

chunks.title: if
chunks.text: Just think  If the welcome function  Deletes all the files of your computer  And you just import a module  And it deletes all the files of your computer

chunks.title: if
chunks.text: Then this is a problem  Isn't it?  It is a problem  It is a very big problem  So

chunks.title: if
chunks.text: This is the thing  So  That's why  You should use if name equals to main  If you know

chunks.title: if
chunks.text: That you can import this module  Like when I am writing harry.py  So if I think  That I will import harry.py  In some other file

chunks.title: if
chunks.text: Then this thing is necessary  Listen to another aspect of this thing  If you are importing such a file  In which you think  That functions are running here and there

chunks.title: if

chunks.title: if
chunks.text: Because of importing  Definitely it will be  Because if underscore underscore name equals to equals to  Is not in main  So that's why you should check them too

chunks.title: if
chunks.text: This is the second aspect  That if you are importing any file  If it is not a built-in module  There is no recognizable module  There is a file given by a friend

chunks.title: if
chunks.text: Which you are importing in your script  Remember that it can be executed  That friend's script  Only by importing  Only you will import and some functions can be executed

chunks.title: if
